content:
Artificial Intelligence

Entrepreneurship

Blogging

Make Money Online

Web Development

6 min read
1 hour ago

--

No coding degree. No developer. No budget. Just an AI, a laptop, and the audacity to try anyway.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let me open with the numbers — because that’s why you’re here.
₹85,000 — what a developer quoted me to build this website.
₹0 — what I actually spent building it.
3 weeks — how long it took me, a person who had never written a single line of code.
35 seconds → 2.8 seconds — my PageSpeed load time, after I fixed it myself.
14 live articles — published, indexed, ranking.
$17 — price of my first digital product, already selling.
$0.01 → target $500/day — my income trajectory, in brutally honest public.
I’m Ayushi. I’m a digital marketer from Gurgaon, India. I have zero coding background. And I built pinkandochre.com — a full Next.js website deployed on Vercel with schema markup, hreflang tags, canonical SEO, and a PageSpeed score above 90 — entirely using Claude AI.
This is not a humble brag. This is a warning. Because what I accidentally discovered while building this site changes everything about who gets to make money on the internet in 2026.

First — Why ₹85,000 Was Never Going to Happen
I had a vision. A specific vision. Not a WordPress blog with a Canva logo slapped on it. A premium editorial lifestyle website — sticky header, reading progress bar, two-column layout, hero sections that felt like a magazine, schema markup for SEO, the whole thing.
I approached two developers.
Developer 1: ₹85,000. Six-week timeline. “Basic SEO not included.”
Developer 2: Never replied.
I had ₹0 allocated for this. I had a job. I had a vision. I had nothing else.
So I did the only logical thing: I opened Claude AI and typed — “I want to build a lifestyle blog. I don’t know how to code. I don’t know what Next.js is. Can we start from zero?”
Claude said yes.

The 3 Weeks That Changed Everything
Week 1 — I described things like a child. Claude built them like an engineer.
“I want the header to stick to the top when I scroll.” “I want a coloured bar that fills up as the reader scrolls through the article.” “I want the homepage hero to feel like opening a magazine.”
Every single sentence became code. I copy-pasted it into VS Code — which I had installed 20 minutes earlier. I pushed it to GitHub — after a YouTube video explained what “push” meant. Vercel deployed it automatically.
I had a live website in 4 days.
Week 2 — I broke everything spectacularly.
PageSpeed score: 12/100.
Twelve. Out of one hundred. My website loaded in 35.6 seconds. I had somehow built the slowest website on the internet.
Back to Claude. Described every error message exactly as it appeared. Got back fixes I didn’t understand but applied anyway. Image optimisation. Lazy loading. Component restructuring. LCP reduction.
35.6 seconds → 8 seconds → 2.8 seconds.
I fixed a performance problem I couldn’t even name, using solutions I couldn’t fully explain, and it worked.
Week 3 — I became dangerously ambitious.
Schema markup on every article — BlogPosting, FAQPage, HowTo, Recipe schema. Hreflang implementation across 20 pages. 301 redirects from old broken URLs. Canonical tags. Internal linking across 14 articles. Google Search Console setup and error fixes.
Things I had read about for three years as a digital marketer — but never implemented because implementation required a developer — were suddenly things I could do. On a Tuesday afternoon. For free.

What I Built — And What It’s Already Doing
Pink & Ochre is a premium lifestyle blog covering fashion, beauty, wellness, food, and books — written specifically for women who are exhausted by content that wasn’t made for them.
Here’s what’s live right now and already getting traffic:
🧘 Wellness Low Cortisol Morning Routine — Why your phone is spiking your stress hormones before 8am and the 7 habits that fix it. This article ranks for “low cortisol morning routine” and is my highest-traffic page.
✨ Skincare Glass Skin Guide — The HER Method — A 6-month tested protocol for South Asian and warm-toned skin. Not written for someone in Seoul with perfect lighting. Written for real Indian skin, tested on mine.
👗 Fashion Capsule Wardrobe Guide 2026–30 pieces, infinite outfits, zero morning panic. The guide I wish existed when I was buying clothes I never wore.
🍽️ Food Aesthetic Grazing Board Ideas — The most-saved article on the site. Because apparently everyone has had a grazing board emergency and nobody talks about it.
🍊 DIY Skincare Orange Peel Powder Recipes — 6 tested DIY recipes using the ingredient you throw in the bin every week. My skin has never looked better. My trash has never been more useful.
14 articles. All SEO-optimised. All with schema markup. All built without a developer.

The Income Stack I’m Building — Publicly
Here is the part most creators won’t tell you. I will, because I think transparency is more useful than pretending I’ve already made it.
Current monthly income from Pink & Ochre: approximately $20–$80
Mostly affiliate commissions. Occasional PayPal tips from readers. The site launched in June 2026. This is month one of monetisation.
Here is what the income stack looks like right now and where it’s going:
Affiliate marketing — ACTIVE I’m in the Dub marketplace. Framer, Beehiiv, Canva, Hostinger affiliate links are live in articles. Every article that mentions a tool has an affiliate link. Commission per sale: $10–$100 depending on product.
Digital products — ACTIVE I sell a 50-prompt Claude AI pack on Payhip for $17. I have two books on Amazon KDP — Claude AI for Non-Coders and Claude AI untuk Orang Awam — both live and selling.
Google AdSense — ACTIVE Publisher code embedded in the Next.js layout. Earning small amounts. Upgrading to Ezoic at 10,000 monthly sessions (target: month 6).
Sponsored posts — PIPELINE Outreach started to Indian skincare and wellness brands. First paid collaboration target: month 4.
Medium Partner Program — BUILDING Publishing canonical versions of Pink & Ochre articles on Medium. Each article points traffic back to the website. You are reading one right now.
Target by month 12: $1,000–$3,000/month Target by month 24: $3,000–$8,000/month
Ambitious? Yes. Impossible? I built a Next.js website with zero coding skills in three weeks. I’ve recalibrated what I think is impossible.

The Thing That Broke My Brain
At the end of week three, I published my first article on my self-built, self-SEO’d, self-deployed website.
PageSpeed 91. Schema markup validating. Canonical tag set. Internal links working. Hreflang across 20 pages.
I sat back and felt something completely unexpected.
Grief.
Not pride. Not excitement. Grief — for every year I spent believing that building things on the internet was someone else’s job. For every idea I had that I shelved because “I’m not technical.” For every project I pitched to developers who didn’t reply or quoted me prices I couldn’t afford.
The barrier was never really technical. It was psychological. And I had been paying that psychological tax for years without knowing it.

The Uncomfortable Number at the End of All This
Here is what the developer would have cost me over time, if I had gone that route:

• Website build: ₹85,000

• Each article upload and formatting: ₹500–1,000

• Schema markup implementation: ₹15,000

• PageSpeed optimisation: ₹10,000

• SEO fixes and redirects: ₹20,000

• Ongoing maintenance per month: ₹5,000–10,000
Conservative 12-month total: ₹2,00,000+
What I actually spent: a Claude AI subscription and more tabs than my laptop could comfortably handle.
Every rupee I didn’t spend on a developer is a rupee that goes directly into the business. And every dollar I earn from this site is proof that the internet has genuinely changed — that creation is no longer gatekept by technical skill or budget.

If You Are Sitting on an Idea Right Now
This is the paragraph I wish someone had written for me two years ago.
You do not need to know how to code. You do not need ₹85,000. You do not need a developer who replies to emails. You do not need to wait until you feel ready, because that feeling does not arrive before you start — only after.
You need clarity about what you want to build. You need an AI that will build it with you. And you need the stubbornness to keep going when your PageSpeed score is 12 and you have no idea what LCP means.
Pink & Ochre exists because I had all three.
Come read it. Come watch me build it in public. Come back in 12 months when the income numbers are less embarrassing.

If this article made you feel something — even just a little less stuck — please give it some claps 👏. It costs nothing and it tells Medium to put this in front of someone else who needs to read it.
Follow me here: https://medium.com/@ayushi.parmar253 My blog: pinkandochre.com My books on Amazon KDP: search “Claude AI for Non-Coders”
